questions.question: Which antimitochondrial antibody subtype is most specific for primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The anti-M2 antibody is the most specific subtype for primary biliary cirrhosis, directed against the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex of the inner mitochondrial membrane.

questions.question: What is the main antigen recognized by the anti-M2 antibody?
questions.explanation: The anti-M2 antibody specifically recognizes the pyruvate dehydrogenase (E2) complex, located in the inner mitochondrial membrane, key in the pathogenesis of primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What laboratory technique is used to detect anti-M2 antibodies?
questions.explanation: Indirect immunofluorescence is the standard technique to detect antimitochondrial antibodies, including anti-M2, showing a granular cytoplasmic pattern in HEp-2 cells.

questions.question: What disease is typically associated with the presence of anti-M2 antibodies?
questions.explanation: The anti-M2 antibody is a highly specific serological marker for primary biliary cirrhosis, present in more than 90% of patients with this autoimmune liver disease.

questions.question: What is the sensitivity of anti-M2 antibody in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The anti-M2 antibody has a sensitivity greater than 90% for primary biliary cirrhosis, being the most sensitive and specific serological marker for this disease.

questions.question: What first-line treatment is indicated in primary biliary cirrhosis with positive anti-M2?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the first-line treatment for primary biliary cirrhosis, improving liver function and delaying disease progression in anti-M2 positive patients.

questions.question: What is the most characteristic biochemical marker of cholestasis in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase is the enzyme that is most elevated in cholestasis, reflecting biliary duct damage, typical of primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What is the most specific antibody for the diagnosis of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The anti-mitochondrial antibody subtype M2 (AMA M2) is the most specific serological marker, present in more than 95% of cases of primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What first-line drug is used to treat cholestasis in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) is the approved first-line treatment for primary biliary cirrhosis, improving liver biochemistry and delaying progression.

questions.question: What is the most common initial symptom in patients with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Pruritus is the most common initial symptom, often intense and predominantly nocturnal, secondary to the accumulation of bile acids in the skin.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The progressive disappearance of the interlobular bile ducts (ductopenia) is the hallmark histological lesion of primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What late complication is associated with untreated primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Advanced primary biliary cirrhosis can cause portal hypertension, hepatic encephalopathy, and an increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma, all of which are late complications.

questions.question: What is the autoimmune disease most frequently associated with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome is the most common autoimmune disease in patients with primary biliary cirrhosis, affecting up to 70-80% of cases, due to a shared immune response against exocrine glands and bile ducts.

questions.question: Which autoimmune thyroid disorder is most frequently associated with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Hashimoto's thyroiditis is the most prevalent autoimmune thyroid disease in patients with primary biliary cirrhosis, due to the overlapping mechanisms of organ-specific autoimmunity that affect both the thyroid gland and the bile ducts.

questions.question: Which of the following antibodies is characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis and may also appear in other associated autoimmune diseases?
questions.explanation: Antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are the distinctive serological marker of primary biliary cirrhosis, present in more than 95% of patients, and can be detected in other associated autoimmune diseases such as Sjögren's syndrome or scleroderma.

questions.question: What cutaneous manifestation of scleroderma is frequently associated with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: CREST syndrome (calcinosis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility, sclerodactyly and telangiectasias) in its incomplete form is the most characteristic manifestation of scleroderma associated with primary biliary cirrhosis, sharing autoantibodies such as anti-centromere.

questions.question: In patients with primary biliary cirrhosis, which of the following hematological autoimmune diseases is most common?
questions.explanation: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) is the autoimmune cytopenia most frequently associated with primary biliary cirrhosis, probably due to common immune dysregulation leading to antibody-mediated platelet destruction.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs used in primary biliary cirrhosis is also used in associated autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: Methotrexate is an immunosuppressant used in rheumatoid arthritis and, although it is not first-line in primary biliary cirrhosis, it has been used in refractory cases or with associated autoimmune manifestations, due to its modulating effect on the immune response.

questions.question: What is the main pathophysiological mechanism of pruritus in primary biliary cirrhosis?
options: Deposit of immunoglobulins in the skin
questions.explanation: Pruritus in primary biliary cirrhosis is mainly associated with the retention and accumulation of bile acids in the blood, which stimulate nerve endings in the skin.

questions.question: What first-line treatment is used for pruritus in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Cholestyramine is an ion exchange resin that binds to bile acids in the intestine, reducing their reabsorption and relieving pruritus in primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: Which laboratory is usually elevated in patients with pruritus due to primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase is the most characteristic cholestatic marker in primary biliary cirrhosis, reflecting chronic biliary obstruction and contributing to pruritus due to the accumulation of bile acids.

questions.question: Which antibody is highly specific for primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are found in more than 90% of patients with primary biliary cirrhosis and are the most specific serological marker for its diagnosis.

questions.question: What is the most common initial symptom in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Generalized pruritus is the most common initial symptom in primary biliary cirrhosis, present in up to 80% of patients at the time of diagnosis, often before jaundice.

questions.question: What histological finding is characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Inflammation and destruction of the interlobular bile ducts (non-suppurative destructive cholangitis) is the classic histological finding of primary biliary cirrhosis, associated with cholestatic pruritus.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains chronic fatigue in primary biliary cirrhosis?
options: Tissue hypoxia due to iron deficiency anemia
Cholestasis and accumulation of bile salts
questions.explanation: In primary biliary cirrhosis, chronic cholestasis leads to accumulation of bile salts and other substances that alter central neurotransmission, significantly contributing to fatigue.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for fatigue associated with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the first-line treatment in primary biliary cirrhosis, improving cholestasis and, in some patients, reducing fatigue, although it is not specific for this symptom.

questions.question: What scale is used to evaluate the impact of fatigue on quality of life in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The Krupp Fatigue Severity Scale is the most used in studies of primary biliary cirrhosis to quantify fatigue and its impact on quality of life.

questions.question: What factor is associated with greater intensity of fatigue in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Intense pruritus is associated with greater fatigue in primary biliary cirrhosis due to the impact on sleep and quality of life, generating a vicious cycle of discomfort and exhaustion.

questions.question: What central mechanism has been proposed in the fatigue of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Dysfunction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis has been proposed in primary biliary cirrhosis, altering the stress response and contributing to chronic central fatigue.

questions.question: Which non-pharmacological intervention has the most evidence to improve fatigue in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Supervised aerobic exercise has been shown to improve fatigue and quality of life in patients with primary biliary cirrhosis, by reducing physical deconditioning and improving muscle function.

questions.question: What is the main mechanism of action of ursodeoxycholic acid in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid stimulates bile secretion, reduces the concentration of hydrophobic bile acids and protects cholangiocytes from damage, being the first-line treatment in primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What dose of ursodeoxycholic acid is recommended as standard treatment in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The standard dose of ursodeoxycholic acid in primary biliary cirrhosis is 13-15 mg/kg/day, administered in several doses, since lower doses do not achieve the desired biochemical efficacy.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs is considered first-line for the treatment of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the only drug approved as first line for primary biliary cirrhosis, improving liver biochemistry and delaying the histological progression of the disease.

questions.question: What biochemical parameter is used to evaluate the response to treatment with ursodeoxycholic acid in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Serum alkaline phosphatase is the primary marker for assessing response to ursodeoxycholic acid; a significant decrease is associated with a better prognosis in primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What is an adverse effect commonly associated with the use of ursodeoxycholic acid?
questions.explanation: Mild diarrhea is the most common adverse effect of ursodeoxycholic acid, although it is generally transient and does not require discontinuation of treatment.

questions.question: At what stage of primary biliary cirrhosis is it recommended to start treatment with ursodeoxycholic acid?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid should be started at any early stage of primary biliary cirrhosis, as it is most effective in slowing progression when given before advanced liver damage.

questions.question: What is the main cause of portal hypertension in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: In primary biliary cirrhosis, progressive liver fibrosis increases resistance to portal flow, raising pressure in the portal system.

questions.question: What is the first ultrasound sign of portal hypertension in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Portal vein dilation (>13 mm) is usually the initial ultrasound finding of portal hypertension, even before varicose veins or ascites appear.

questions.question: Which complication of primary biliary cirrhosis is directly associated with portal hypertension?
questions.explanation: Gastroesophageal varices are a direct consequence of portal hypertension, as venous collaterals form that attempt to divert the obstructed flow.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for primary biliary cirrhosis with risk of portal hypertension?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the drug of choice for primary biliary cirrhosis, as it delays the progression of fibrosis and reduces the risk of portal hypertension.

questions.question: What value of the hepatic venous pressure gradient indicates clinically significant portal hypertension?
questions.explanation: A hepatic venous pressure gradient ≥10 mmHg defines clinically significant portal hypertension, associated with the risk of varicose veins and decompensation.

questions.question: Which of the following serum markers is typically elevated in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase is the most characteristic cholestatic marker in primary biliary cirrhosis, reflecting damage to the intrahepatic bile ducts.

questions.question: Which of the following antibodies is most characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are the classic serological marker of primary biliary cirrhosis, with very high sensitivity and specificity, unlike other antibodies that are associated with other autoimmune liver diseases.

questions.question: Which cholangiographic finding is typical of primary sclerosing cholangitis?
options: Cystic dilation of the common bile duct
questions.explanation: Cholangiography in primary sclerosing cholangitis shows multifocal stenosis and saccular dilations that give a rosary appearance, while primary biliary cirrhosis does not present characteristic cholangiographic alterations.

questions.question: What is the first line of pharmacological treatment in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the only drug approved for the treatment of primary biliary cirrhosis, as it improves liver biochemistry and delays disease progression, unlike other agents that do not have sufficient evidence.

questions.question: What inflammatory bowel disease is most frequently associated with primary sclerosing cholangitis?
questions.explanation: Ulcerative colitis is the inflammatory bowel disease most frequently associated with primary sclerosing cholangitis, present in up to 80% of cases, while Crohn's disease has a much lower association.

questions.question: Which of the following laboratory tests is usually most elevated in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Alkaline phosphatase is the enzyme that is most elevated in primary biliary cirrhosis due to chronic cholestatic damage, reflecting obstruction of the small bile ducts, while transaminases are usually less elevated.

questions.question: What histological finding is distinctive of primary biliary cirrhosis in the early stages?
questions.explanation: In the initial stages of primary biliary cirrhosis, non-suppurative inflammation and destruction of the interlobular bile ducts are observed, forming granulomas, unlike sclerosing cholangitis, which affects larger ducts.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of liver granulomas in biopsy?
questions.explanation: Hepatic granulomas are classically associated with primary biliary cirrhosis, present in up to 60% of biopsies in early stages.

questions.question: What type of granuloma is typically seen in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Noncaseating granulomas, composed of epithelioid histiocytes, are characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis on liver biopsy.

questions.question: Which serum antibody supports the diagnosis of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Antimitochondrial antibody (AMA) is the most specific serological marker of primary biliary cirrhosis, present in >90% of cases.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the only approved drug that improves liver biochemistry and delays histological progression in primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: What histological stage of primary biliary cirrhosis presents florid granulomas?
questions.explanation: Stage I (portal) is characterized by inflammatory infiltrate and non-caseating granulomas around the bile ducts, before extensive fibrosis.

questions.question: What histological finding is distinctive of advanced primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The progressive loss of interlobular bile ducts (ductopenia) is characteristic of primary biliary cirrhosis, especially in advanced stages.

questions.question: What is the main cause of vitamin D deficiency in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: In primary biliary cirrhosis, cholestasis reduces the secretion of bile acids into the intestine, which alters the absorption of fats and fat-soluble vitamins such as vitamin D, generating a deficiency.

questions.question: Which fat-soluble vitamin is most frequently associated with neuropathy due to its deficiency in chronic cholestasis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin E deficiency can cause peripheral neuropathy and ataxia due to axonal degeneration, being a relevant neurological complication in patients with chronic cholestasis and fat malabsorption.

questions.question: What laboratory finding is characteristic of vitamin K deficiency due to cholestasis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin K deficiency alters the hepatic synthesis of coagulation factors II, VII, IX and X, prolonging the prothrombin time, which is the most sensitive marker of this deficiency in cholestasis.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for fat-soluble vitamin deficiency in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Parenteral supplementation of fat-soluble vitamins is the first line when there is severe malabsorption due to cholestasis, since the oral route does not guarantee adequate absorption due to the lack of intestinal bile acids.

questions.question: What clinical manifestation is typically associated with vitamin A deficiency in chronic cholestasis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin A is essential for retinal function; Its deficiency due to malabsorption in cholestasis causes night blindness, which is an early and reversible manifestation with adequate supplementation.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism explains osteomalacia in patients with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency secondary to cholestasis reduces intestinal absorption of calcium and phosphorus, altering bone mineralization and causing osteomalacia, a common skeletal complication in this disease.

questions.question: What is the prevalence of primary biliary cirrhosis in middle-aged women?
questions.explanation: Primary biliary cirrhosis has a marked female predominance, with approximately 90% of cases diagnosed in women, especially between 40 and 60 years of age.

questions.question: What factor is most associated with the development of primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Primary biliary cirrhosis is an autoimmune cholestatic disease with a strong genetic predisposition, not related to viral infections, alcohol or toxins as the primary cause.

questions.question: What is the typical age of diagnosis in women with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Primary biliary cirrhosis is typically diagnosed in middle-aged women, between 40 and 60 years old, and is rare in children, adolescents, or very old people.

questions.question: What clinical manifestation is most common in women with primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: In early stages of primary biliary cirrhosis, pruritus and fatigue are cardinal symptoms, while severe obstructive jaundice and ascites are late.

questions.question: What is the first-line treatment for primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Ursodeoxycholic acid is the only approved drug that improves liver biochemistry and delays progression in primary biliary cirrhosis, being the first-line treatment.

questions.question: What antibody is characteristic in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are the most specific serological marker of primary biliary cirrhosis, present in more than 90% of patients.

questions.question: What is the main indication for liver transplantation in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: In primary biliary cirrhosis, severe portal hypertension with complications such as ascites or variceal hemorrhage is an established indication for liver transplantation in advanced stages.

questions.question: What scoring system is used to prioritize liver transplantation in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The MELD model (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease) is the standard system for prioritizing liver transplantation in advanced primary biliary cirrhosis.

questions.question: Which of the following findings contraindicate liver transplantation in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Unresectable cholangiocarcinoma is an absolute contraindication for liver transplantation due to the high risk of post-transplant tumor recurrence.

questions.question: What minimum MELD score usually indicates the need for liver transplantation in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: A MELD score between 15 and 20 generally suggests that the patient with primary biliary cirrhosis benefits from liver transplantation.

questions.question: What is the expected 5-year post-transplant survival in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: The 5-year survival after liver transplantation for primary biliary cirrhosis is approximately 85-90%, with excellent long-term results.

questions.question: What post-transplant complication is most common in primary biliary cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Recurrence of primary biliary cirrhosis can occur in up to 20-30% of post-transplant patients, being the most characteristic complication.
